topic: Carlos trying to grow cilantro — comedic failure

turns.content: Let me tell you what my husband has done now. Carlos has decided he is going to grow cilantro. In Miami. In March. The man retired from a career where he ran into burning buildings, and now his greatest enemy is an herb.

Cilantro in Miami in March is actually a real challenge — it bolts very quickly in warm weather, meaning it shoots up a tall stalk and flowers instead of producing those leafy greens you want for cooking. Cilantro prefers cooler temperatures, ideally between 50 and 80 degrees. Once it gets consistently above 80, it basically gives up on being useful.

How far along is he? Has he planted seeds, or is he working with transplants?

He has tried both. First he bought transplants from the nursery on Coral Way. They lasted about six days before they turned into sad little sticks with flowers on top. So then he said — and I am quoting — 'I am going to start from seed and do it right this time.' He bought three different brands of seeds. Three.

The dedication is admirable, even if the strategy needs some work. The transplants bolting in six days is classic — cilantro doesn't transplant well in heat because the root disturbance stresses the plant and accelerates bolting.

Starting from seed is actually the better approach, so he's on the right track there. But three brands of seed won't help if the growing conditions are the same. The variety that matters is the type — he should look for slow-bolt cilantro varieties like 'Calypso' or 'Santo.' These are bred to tolerate warmer conditions and take longer to flower.

Where is he planting them? Full sun, part shade?

Full sun. On the back patio. Which gets approximately one thousand hours of direct sunlight every day. I told him the plants looked like they were suffering and he said, 'Rosa, they need sun. Everything needs sun.' As if he is now a botanist.

That is almost certainly the problem. In South Florida, full afternoon sun will cook cilantro — it's not a tropical plant, it's a cool-season Mediterranean herb. He needs to move the operation to a spot that gets morning sun but afternoon shade. East-facing exposure is ideal.

If the back patio is all he has, he could use a shade cloth — they sell them at any garden center. A 40-50% shade cloth over the pots during the afternoon would drop the temperature and light intensity enough to give the cilantro a fighting chance.

Also, watering in the morning rather than the evening helps keep roots cool. And shallow, wide containers are better than deep pots because cilantro has a shallow root system.
A shade cloth. I will tell him, but he is going to resist. He has a very specific vision for his herb garden and it does not include what he will call 'a tarp over my plants.' The man has his pride.

Understood. If the shade cloth is too much of an aesthetic compromise, here's the diplomatic alternative: suggest he move the cilantro pots under the edge of a tree canopy, or next to a taller plant that provides natural afternoon shade. He gets his garden looking the way he wants, the cilantro gets the protection it needs, and nobody has to use the word 'tarp.'

Another option — if he's truly committed to the patio location — is to plant in succession. Sow a small batch of seeds every two weeks. Each batch will eventually bolt, but he'll always have a younger crop coming up behind it. Professional herb growers in warm climates do exactly this. It's not admitting defeat, it's strategy.

Succession planting. That is actually clever. He would like that because it sounds like a military operation and Carlos responds well to anything that sounds tactical. I will present it that way. 'Carlos, you need to deploy in waves.'

That is perfect framing. Deploy in waves, stagger the platoons, always have the next unit ready. Every two weeks, a fresh row of seeds goes in. By the time the first batch bolts, the second batch is ready to harvest. Continuous supply, no downtime.

And honestly, the coriander seeds from the bolted plants are useful too — they're the spice, and they're excellent in marinades and soups. So even his failures are producing something. He can think of the bolted plants as reconnaissance missions that brought back intelligence.

Reconnaissance missions. Dios mio, you are as bad as he is. Now tell me something — is there anything else he could grow out there that would actually cooperate with this climate? He has the pots, he has the soil, he has the enthusiasm. I would rather redirect his energy than watch him fight a losing war with one herb.

Absolutely. South Florida is actually excellent for growing herbs — just not the cool-season ones in summer. Here's what will thrive on that sunny patio with minimal fuss:

- **Cuban oregano** — not true oregano, but it grows like a weed in Miami heat. It's a succulent-type plant, very forgiving, and great in black beans and mojo.
- **Culantro** (recao) — not cilantro, but a close relative with a similar flavor that actually prefers tropical heat. Used heavily in Caribbean and Latin cooking. It grows flat, leafy, and doesn't bolt the way cilantro does.
- **Thai basil or sweet basil** — loves full sun and heat. He'll have more basil than he knows what to do with.
- **Mint** — almost impossible to kill. Put it in its own pot or it will take over everything else.

Culantro especially might satisfy his cilantro craving. The flavor is similar but stronger, and it will actually survive his patio.

Culantro. I know culantro — my mother used it. She would be very amused that her son-in-law is out here struggling with cilantro when culantro was the answer all along. I will tell him about it, but I will let him discover it himself. Ay, he will act like he invented it.

He absolutely will. He'll bring you a leaf, hold it up like a trophy, and say something like 'Rosa, I found something better.' Let him have the moment. Meanwhile, you'll know your mother had it figured out decades ago.

The Cuban oregano is also worth putting in his hands — it's practically indestructible, and once he has a success or two under his belt, the cilantro setbacks won't sting as much. A man needs a win in the garden before he can handle a loss.

A man needs a win. That is the truth. Fine — I will pass all of this along. The slow-bolt variety, morning sun, succession planting, and the culantro backup plan. And if none of it works, I will go to Sedano's and buy cilantro for forty cents a bunch like a rational person.

That is always the backup plan, and an excellent one. But let him try — there's something satisfying about growing your own herbs, even in a hostile climate. And if he does eventually produce a usable bunch of cilantro from that patio, you will never hear the end of it. Be prepared for that.

Good luck to both of you. The general and his cilantro campaign.
